title: Interactive visualization of 2-D persistence modules
speaker: Michael Lesnick
abstract: In topological data analysis, we often study data by associating to the data a filtered topological space, whose structure we can then examine using persistent homology. However, in many settings, a single filtered space is not a rich enough invariant to encode the interesting structure of our data. This motivates the study of multidimensional persistence, which associates to the data a topological space simultaneously equipped with two or more filtrations.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Project goal and gap in multidimensional persistence tools
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames multidimensional persistence as a natural multiparameter extension of barcode-based persistent homology and identifies the lack of practical two-dimensional visualization tools as the gap addressed by the joint work.

proof_stages.label: One-parameter persistence pipeline
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the standard route from data to a filtration, then to a persistence module via homology, and finally to a barcode through the interval-decomposition theorem.

proof_stages.label: Outlier failure and density-sensitive bifiltrations
proof_stages.summary: After recalling ordinary stability and its failure under outliers, the talk introduces bifiltrations and two-dimensional persistence modules, especially the co-density Rips bifiltration that varies both scale and density threshold.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic obstruction to a direct two-dimensional barcode
proof_stages.summary: Krull-Schmidt decomposition still gives indecomposable summands for two-dimensional persistence modules, but quiver-theoretic complexity shows that these summands are far too complicated to serve as interval-like visual barcodes.

proof_stages.label: Replacement invariants for interactive visualization
proof_stages.summary: Because a full two-dimensional barcode is not usable, the tool visualizes dimension functions, barcodes of affine one-dimensional slices, and multigraded Betti numbers, with stable diagonal slices supporting exploratory analysis.

proof_stages.label: Augmented-arrangement data structure for real-time slice queries
proof_stages.summary: The algorithmic framework precomputes an augmented line arrangement and barcode templates so that the barcode of a queried slice is obtained quickly by pushing stored endpoint pairs onto the selected line, alongside fast computation of multigraded Betti numbers.

examples.name: Circle destroyed by low-density outliers
examples.note: Adding even a few outliers to a sampled circle can fill the Rips hole and erase the long bar, visibly forcing the move toward density-sensitive multiparameter persistence.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the outlier circle example, explaining how adding low-density points makes the Rips complex fill the hole at small scales and removes the long bar
